1: שִׁירוּ לַיהוָה שִׁיר חָדָשׁ שִׁירוּ לַיהוָה כָּל-הָאָרֶץ (shîrû layhwāh shîr ḥādāsh shîrû layhwāh kol-hāʾāreṣ, "Sing to the LORD a new song; sing to the LORD, all the earth!") — shîr ḥādāsh: the new song (Ps 33:3; 40:3; 98:1; 144:9; 149:1; Isa 42:10; Rev 5:9 — the eschatological hymn that only the redeemed can sing). The repetition of the imperative shîrû is not redundant: the first is the command, the second extends the address from Israel to kol-hāʾāreṣ (all the earth — the universal scope that is the driving theme of Ps 96). Psalm 96 is nearly identical to 1 Chronicles 16:23-33 (the song David appointed when the ark arrived in Jerusalem) — the enthronement psalm given a historical anchor in the Davidic establishment of worship.
2: שִׁירוּ לַיהוָה בָּרְכוּ שְׁמוֹ בַּשְּׂרוּ מִיּוֹם-לְיוֹם יְשׁוּעָתוֹ (shîrû layhwāh bārĕkhû shĕmô basshĕrû miyyôm-lĕyôm yĕshûʿātô, "Sing to the LORD, bless his name; announce his salvation from day to day!") — bassar (piel: to announce good news — the same verb behind mĕbaśśêr in Isa 52:7: "how beautiful the feet of one who announces good news"; the Greek εὐαγγελίζεσθαι/evangelism). Miyyôm-lĕyôm: day to day (the continuous proclamation — not a single announcement but a daily rhythm of gospel-bearing). Yĕshûʿāh: salvation (the name of Joshua/Jesus: Num 13:16; Matt 1:21).
3: סַפְּרוּ בַגּוֹיִם כְּבוֹדוֹ בְּכָל-הָעַמִּים נִפְלְאֹתָיו (sappĕrû baggôyim kĕbôdô bĕkhol-hāʿammîm nipĕlĕʾōtāyw, "Announce his glory among the nations and his wondrous deeds among all peoples!") — sappĕrû (piel: to recount, to declare in full — not a brief mention but a thorough narration). Kĕbôdô: his glory (the kābôd YHWH — the weighty, heavy reality of his divine presence and character). Nipĕlĕʾōtāyw: his wonders (from pālāʾ: extraordinary acts that exceed human comprehension — Exod 3:20; 15:11; Ps 77:11). The missionaries's mandate: not opinions about God but the narration of his actual acts.
4: כִּי גָדוֹל יְהוָה וּמְהֻלָּל מְאֹד נוֹרָא הוּא עַל-כָּל-אֱלֹהִים (kî gādôl yĕhwāh ûmĕhullāl mĕʾōd nôrāʾ hûʾ ʿal-kol-ʾĕlōhîm, "For the LORD is great and most worthy of praise; he is to be held in awe above every other god!") — gādôl: great in every superlative (size, power, goodness — Ps 145:3). Mĕhullāl mĕʾōd: greatly to be praised (hallel: the root of Hallelujah). Nôrāʾ: awesome/terrifying (from yārēʾ: to fear — the word for awe-inspiring). ʿAl-kol-ʾĕlōhîm: above all gods — the comparative claim that relativizes all rivals without denying their existence as spiritual realities (Ps 82:1; 1 Cor 8:5-6).
5: כִּי כָּל-אֱלֹהֵי הָעַמִּים אֱלִילִים (kî kol-ʾĕlōhê hāʿammîm ʾĕlîlîm, "For all the gods of the nations are ʾĕlîlîm!") — ʾĕlîlîm: a pointed theological term — "worthless things, nothings" (from the root related to ʾal: no/not; the word plays on ʾĕlōhîm but inverts it). Isa 2:8,18,20; 10:10; 19:3 use the same word. The contrast is cosmic: all rival "gods" are zeroes; YHWH made the heavens. וַיהוָה שָׁמַיִם עָשָׂה (wayhwāh shāmayim ʿāśāh, "but the LORD made the heavens") — the creator vs the creature: idols are things made by human hands (Ps 115:4-8); YHWH is the Maker of the sky itself.
6: הוֹד-וְהָדָר לְפָנָיו עֹז וְתִפְאֶרֶת בְּמִקְדָּשׁוֹ (hôd-wĕhādār lĕpānāyw ʿōz wĕtipʾeret bĕmiqdāshô, "Honor and majesty are before him; strength and beauty fill his sanctuary!") — two paired couplets: hôd wĕhādār (honor and majesty — the royal radiance of the divine presence; Ps 104:1; 111:3) and ʿōz wĕtipʾeret (strength and beauty/splendor — the two-fold character of the sanctuary). Lĕpānāyw: before him (in his very presence these qualities exist as attendants). Bĕmiqdāshô: in his sanctuary — bringing the cosmic divine character into the concrete worship space.
7: הָבוּ לַיהוָה מִשְׁפְּחוֹת עַמִּים הָבוּ לַיהוָה כָּבוֹד וָעֹז (hābû layhwāh mishpĕḥôt ʿammîm hābû layhwāh kābôd wāʿōz, "Give to the LORD, O families of the nations — give to the LORD glory and strength!") — hābû: give!/ascribe! (imperative of yāhab: to give — Ps 29:1-2 uses the same structure for the heavenly beings). Mishpĕḥôt ʿammîm: the families/clans of the peoples — a more intimate, household-level word for nations than gôyim, implying the universal call extends to every family unit. The glory they ascribe already belongs to God; they are acknowledging what is true, not granting something new.
8: הָבוּ לַיהוָה כְּבוֹד שְׁמוֹ שְׂאוּ-מִנְחָה וּבֹאוּ לְחַצְרוֹתָיו (hābû layhwāh kĕbôd shĕmô śĕʾû-minḥāh ûbōʾû lĕḥaṣrōtāyw, "Give to the LORD the honor his name deserves; bring your offering and come into his courts!") — kĕbôd shĕmô: "the honor/glory of his name" — giving the name its due weight (kābôd: heaviness, weightiness). Minḥāh: the grain offering (Lev 2) or simply "gift/tribute" — the tangible physical expression of honor. Ḥaṣrōtāyw: his courts (the outer courts of the sanctuary open to all who would approach). The nations are invited into the temple courts — Isa 56:7: "my house shall be called a house of prayer for all peoples."
9: הִשְׁתַּחֲווּ לַיהוָה בְּהַדְרַת-קֹדֶשׁ חִילוּ מִפָּנָיו כָּל-הָאָרֶץ (hishtaḥăwû layhwāh bĕhadrat-qōdesh ḥîlû mippānāyw kol-hāʾāreṣ, "Worship the LORD in the splendor of holiness; tremble before him, all the earth!") — bĕhadrat-qōdesh: "in the splendor of holiness" or "in holy array/adornment" — the ambiguity covers both the worshiper's approach (dressed in the beauty of consecrated life) and the sanctuary's character (radiant with the holiness of the divine presence). Ḥîlû: tremble, writhe (the same verb for labor pains — Ps 29:8-9; 77:16 — the earth convulsing in the divine presence). The invitation to draw near and the command to tremble coexist without resolution.
10: אִמְרוּ בַגּוֹיִם יְהוָה מָלָךְ (ʾimrû baggôyim yĕhwāh mālāk, "Declare among the nations: 'The LORD reigns!'") — YHWH mālāk: the enthronement formula (Ps 93:1; 97:1; 99:1) — the proclamation that the cosmic battle has been decided, the true king is on his throne. אַף-תִּכּוֹן תֵּבֵל בַּל-תִּמּוֹט (ʾap-tikkôn têbêl bal-timmôṭ, "The world is set firm and cannot be shaken!") — têbêl: the inhabited world (the earth as the place of human civilization). Tikkôn: established, set firm (as opposed to the primordial chaos). יָדִין עַמִּים בְּמֵישָׁרִים (yādîn ʿammîm bĕmêshārîm, "He will judge the peoples with equity!") — mêshārîm: equity, what is straight/level (Ps 45:6; 99:4). The proclamation of kingship is simultaneously the promise of justice.
11: יִשְׂמְחוּ הַשָּׁמַיִם וְתָגֵל הָאָרֶץ יִרְעַם הַיָּם וּמְלֹאוֹ (yiśmĕḥû hashshāmayim wĕtāgêl hāʾāreṣ yirʿam hayyām ûmĕlōʾô, "Let the heavens rejoice and the earth be glad; let the sea roar with all that fills it!") — the cosmos responds to the enthronement proclamation of v10: heaven above, earth below, and sea between all break into the acclamation. Rāʿam: to thunder, to roar (Ps 77:18 — the roar of the sea-storm as divine theophany). The joy of v11 is not merely emotional — it is creation's proper response to its Creator being acknowledged as king.
12: יַעֲלֹז שָׂדַי וְכָל-אֲשֶׁר-בּוֹ אָז יְרַנְּנוּ כָּל-עֲצֵי-יָעַר (yaʿălōz śāday wĕkhol-ʾăsher-bô ʾāz yĕrannĕnû kol-ʿăṣê-yāʿar, "Let the fields exult and everything in them! Then all the trees of the forest shall sing for joy!") — ʿālaz: to exult, leap for joy (Ps 68:4; Prov 23:16). The celebration widens from humans (vv1-9) to nations (v10) to sky, earth, sea (v11) to fields and forests (v12): a concentric-ring expansion of the worshipping community. The "then" (ʾāz) in v12 points forward to v13's coming — the exultation precedes and anticipates the arrival of the judge.
13: לִפְנֵי יְהוָה כִּי בָא כִּי בָא לִשְׁפֹּט הָאָרֶץ (lipnê yĕhwāh kî bāʾ kî bāʾ lishpōṭ hāʾāreṣ, "before the LORD, for he is coming — yes, he is coming — to judge the earth!") — the double kî bāʾ (for he is coming) is liturgical emphasis — the repeated announcement of an imminent, certain arrival. יִשְׁפֹּט-תֵּבֵל בְּצֶדֶק וְעַמִּים בֶּאֱמוּנָתוֹ (yishpōṭ-têbêl bĕṣedeq wĕʿammîm beʾĕmûnātô, "He will judge the world in righteousness and the peoples in his faithfulness!") — the closing promise: the coming judge brings not arbitrary power but ṣedeq (righteousness — what is morally right) and ʾĕmûnāh (faithfulness — trustworthy consistency with covenant character). The same closing appears at Ps 98:9. The entire creation chorus of vv11-13 is the response to this promise.
